meta.title: Thread Geometry & Standards (Metrik, UN, W, Tr Profiles)
meta.description: A comprehensive guide to thread profiles, angles, and tolerances used in modern manufacturing. Covers ISO Metric, Unified National, Whitworth, and Trapezoidal standards.

hero.h1: Thread Geometry & Standards: The DNA of Mechanical Joints
hero.intro:
Every bolt is a complex mathematical helix. To the untrained eye, they look similar, but the difference between a 60° Metric thread and a 55° Whitworth thread is the difference between a secure joint and a catastrophic failure. This module decodes the geometric standards used in modern CNC machining.

Thread standards encode helix geometry in a compact designation — M16×1.5-6g tells a machinist the nominal diameter, pitch, and tolerance band. CNC threading cycles, tap drill charts, and fastener strength calculations all depend on reading these symbols correctly.

This module aligns shop-floor vocabulary with the Thread Geometry engine in AluCalc, covering metric, UN, Whitworth, and trapezoidal families.

stepByStep: Identify the primary thread system (Metrik vs. Inch).
Verify the profile angle (e.g., 60° for ISO Metrik).
Select the appropriate tolerance class (4h for tight, 6g for standard).
Calculate the major and minor diameters based on the pitch.
Choose the correct machining tool based on the profile.
Calculate stress area As from pitch and pitch diameter for strength checks.
Verify tap drill size against minor diameter before production run.

example.calculation: Major Diameter = 16 mm, Pitch = 1.5 mm, Minor Diameter ≈ 14.16 mm

whyThisMatters: Profile mismatch (e.g., trying to fit a UN into an M hole) destroys threads immediately.
Tolerance grades (6g vs 4h) determine the 'fit' and assembly ease in mass production.
commonMistakes: Mixing up 60° (Metric) and 55° (Whitworth) threads, which look nearly identical.
Ignoring thread tolerances (6g/6H) in critical structural applications.
Using partial profile tools for full-profile requirements.

textbookInsights.insight: Kısmi profil (partial profile) kesici uçlar, aynı uçla farklı hatveleri işleyebilir ancak diş tepesinde keskin kenar bırakır.
Diş hassasiyeti (6g, 4h vb.) imalat kalitesini ve montaj sıkılığını belirleyen en kritik parametredir.

textbookInsights.init: Trapez dişler (Tr) 30° tepe açısına sahiptir ve genellikle güç iletimi için kullanılır.

calculatorCta.label: Design custom thread profiles in the Fastener Lab

faq.question: What is the difference between 6g and 4h tolerances?
faq.answer: 6g is the standard commercial fit, while 4h is a tighter, precision fit used in high-vibration environments.

faq.question: What does 6g/6H mean?
faq.answer: 6g is external thread tolerance (bolt); 6H is internal (nut). Lower numbers mean tighter fits.

faq.question: Why are Whitworth threads 55°?
faq.answer: Historical British standard — incompatible with 60° metric/UN profiles despite similar appearance.

faq.question: When are trapezoidal (Tr) threads used?
faq.answer: Power screws and lead screws where efficiency and load transmission matter more than fast assembly.

learningObjectives: Distinguish 60° metric/UN threads from 55° Whitworth and 30° trapezoidal profiles
Read tolerance designations (6g/6H, 4h) and their assembly implications
Estimate stress area from pitch and major diameter for fastener sizing
keyTakeaways: Profile angle mismatch destroys threads on first assembly
Tolerance class controls fit — 4h is tighter than 6g for critical joints
Partial-profile tools trade flexibility for crest sharpness
